title: Causation-guided mechanism identification and interpretable reduced-order modeling of damage-driving grain-boundary stress in creep
authors: Weichen Kong, Yanwei Dai, Yinglin Zhang, Yinghua Liu
abstract: Grain-boundary (GB) local stress is central to the initiation and evolution of long-term creep damage in polycrystalline superalloys. Owing to the high-dimensional nonlinear relationships between the GB stress response and multiple crystallographic, microstructural, and micromechanical characteristics, it remains challenging to identify the key characteristics governing GB stress and to elucidate their mechanisms of influence. Dislocation-climb-affected crystal-plasticity finite-element simulations of minimal grain clusters are combined with an integrated causation-guided machine-learning framework, in which mechanics-informed descriptors are analyzed by causation entropy to identify governing mechanisms and then distilled into a reduced-order regression form for interpretable prediction of GB normal stress. Among 18 physically motivated characteristics, the GB inclination angle, the slip transmission, the climb-related Schmid-type indicator, and the elastic-modulus mismatch are found to be dominant, revealing the coupled roles of interfacial geometry, crystallographic compatibility, creep stress relaxation, and micromechanical contrast. The identified characteristics hierarchy and functional representation remain effective under multiaxial loading and can be extended to tricrystal systems through physically interpretable nonlocal augmentation when a purely local description becomes insufficient, demonstrating strong physical consistency and robust generalizability across physical conditions. The extracted candidate functions also improve surrogate-model performance across multiple machine-learning model classes, providing supporting evidence for the physical relevance and efficiency of the identified representation. The proposed methods demonstrate strong potential for the development of interpretable machine-learning models and for the study of microscale nonlocal damage.
summary: This work develops a novel, physics-guided machine learning framework to predict local grain boundary stress during creep in superalloys. By combining detailed crystal plasticity simulations with causation entropy analysis, the authors distill complex microstructural data into a reduced-order model. This provides a highly interpretable tool for understanding the fundamental physical mechanisms governing material failure at the microscale.
main_problem: The core challenge is identifying the key physical characteristics that govern the high-dimensional, nonlinear local stress state at grain boundaries during creep in polycrystalline superalloys.
main_result: The study successfully identifies a reduced set of physically relevant, dominant characteristics (like GB inclination angle and elastic mismatch) that allow for the development of an interpretable, reduced-order model for predicting GB normal stress.
method: It combines Crystal Plasticity Finite Element (CPFE) simulations to generate data with an advanced causation-guided machine-learning framework that uses causation entropy for mechanistic feature selection.
model_or_system: The physical system is polycrystalline superalloys undergoing creep deformation. The model involves simulating minimal grain clusters (bicrystal/tricrystal) using CPFE, incorporating dislocation glide and climb mechanisms.
key_observables: Grain Boundary (GB) normal stress ($\sigma_{33}$ or $\sigma_{L33}$), slip transmission factor, GB inclination angle, and various measures of elastic modulus mismatch.
important_parameters: Creep secondary creep regime, multiaxial loading conditions, and the set of 18 physically motivated microstructural and mechanical descriptors.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the governing physics can be captured by a reduced-order representation derived from the identified key functional relationships, and that the causation entropy method correctly isolates causal mechanisms over mere correlation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the application of the framework under uniaxial and multiaxial loading, showing heat maps of top candidate features and comparing the predictive performance of the ML model using original vs. key functions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the physical necessity of the approach (limitations of classical/purely data-driven models), then detailing the CPFE simulation setup and the comprehensive calculation of numerous mechanics-informed descriptors, culminating in the application of causation entropy to build and validate the reduced-order, interpretable model.
why_interesting: While focused on materials science, the methodology—using information theory (causation entropy) to distill complex, high-dimensional physical data into a minimal, interpretable functional form—is highly relevant to developing physics-informed machine learning models in quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
